coredata.dc:description: Airborne transmitted as well as multiresistant pathogens can spread rapidly in high traffic enclosed public spaces. Antimicrobial coatings of surfaces are a promising approach limiting the spread of diseases by targeted contact inhibition of microbes. A novel class of light-activated coatings including hydrophobic carbon quantum dots (hCQDs) and up-converter (UPC) are developed for this purpose including Safe-by-Design (SbD) principles. The efficacy against pathogens and the human safety aspects need to be well balanced. In case of fire-related accidents, these additives should not increase flammability, smoke release or the formation of harmful entities. In this work, antimicrobial hCQDs & up-converters are coated onto porous and non-porous substrates, exploring the interplay between substrates and coatings on the release of fume products after incineration. The airborne soot particles were assessed for their polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbon (PAH) content and their potential to cause a pro-inflammatory cytokine response in human lung cells. No significant acute in vitro lung cytotoxicity was detected from nano-coated surfaces after incineration. With this study, an important intermediate goal is reached and provides the rational for further investigations.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Overview of materials and methods for assessing the safety and sustainability of antimicrobial-coated substrates. (A) Spray-coating setup within a laminar flow cabinet, with a defined spray angle, height, and distance. (B) Analysis of uniformity (homogeneity) of spray-coating of hCQDs dissolved in toluene on various substrates, including plastics and textiles. (C) UV-light-based analysis of the clarity of imprints of the coated areas on the underlying coating paper. (D) SEM imaging indicated that the hCQDs coating was not visible on plastic or textile substrates. (E) Raman analysis confirmed the presence of hCQDs, identifiable as disc-like shapes, on glass slides, plastic, and textile substrates. (F-I) Illustration of the setup and performance of incineration experiments: (F) A 10 cm × 10 cm hCQDs-coated textile sample mounted on the incineration platform's sample holder. (G) A running incineration experiment applied to compare coated and uncoated substrates. (H) In vitro cell culture exposure chamber, equipped with a quartz crystal microbalance, for the exposure to combustion fumes. (I) Example of coated textile samples retrieved after 10 min of burning.

Characterization and functionalization of hCQDs and up-converter-based coatings. (A-I) hCQDs coating spray deposition characterization on plastic and textile. (A) SEM image of uncoated plastic substrate PUC. (B, C, G, H) Raman characterizations of PUC and PQC plastics: (B) Optical images of the PUC with Raman map in the pink inset. (C) Optical image of PQC with Raman map in the green inset. (G) Raman spectrum of the PUC from the multicomponent analysis. (H) Raman spectrum of the PQC from the multicomponent analysis. (D) SEM image of uncoated textile TUC. (E, F, I) Raman characterizations of TUC and TQC textiles: (E) Optical images of the TUC with Raman map in the pink inset. (F) Optical image of TQC with Raman map in the green inset. (I) Raman spectrum of the TQC from the multicomponent analysis. (J-L) Up-converter coating characterization on plastic PEC: (J) SEM top imaging confirming the homogenous distribution of up-converter particles, black, within the white Clearcoat matrix. (K) Side view of Clearcoat matrix with light coloured, round crystal shaped, up-converter particles incorporated. (L&M): Photodynamic evaluation of the coatings. (L) Methylene blue photobleaching of uncoated and up-converter coated surface. (M) Singlet oxygen evaluation from EPR spectra of TEMP oxidation: green, non-coated and blue, hCQDs-coated (frequency of 9.45 GHz).

Data summary of the burning experiments of non-coated versus coated substrates (plastic, textile and wood) applying the Incineration platform. The outer frame divides the substrates into plastic (blue), textile (green) and wood (yellow). The left column illustrates the particle size (5–1000 µm) on the y-axis over the burning time displayed on the x-axis and the amount of particle per time shown (dN/dlogde: 1 ×109 – red; 1 ×106 – green; 1 ×103 - blue) on the z-axis of the DMS measurement. The middle column illustrates the DMS size distribution of particles (5–1000 µm) on the x-axis summarized over the total burning duration of 600 sec (y-axis), of the single burnings [N = 3] and the average of the three single burnings (bold green line). The right column illustrates the FTT cone calorimeter results total heat release HRR (grey; kW m−2) and the total smoke release TSR (black; m2 m−2) on the y-axis and the % CO production (red; %) as well as the % CO2 production (orange; %) on the z-axis over the burning time displayed on the x-axis. The top row shows the results collected for the uncoated plastic substrate (PUC) followed by hCQDs (PQC) and UPC (PEC) coated plastic substrates, uncoated textile substrate (TUC) and hCQDs coated textile (TQC) and uncoated wood substrate (WUC) and UPC (WEC) coated wood.

Polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbon (PAH) quantification of collected incineration particles after exposition chamber in unit's mg PAH per g incineration particles. Analytics were performed applying gas chromatography and coupled single quadrupole mass spectrometer detector. The different detected PAHs derived of coated substrates were illustrated in a bar chart and compared to uncoated control (A, PUC vs. PQC; B, TUC vs. TQC; C, PUC vs. PEC; D, WUC vs. WEC). Detected PAH's include from top to bottom: Dibenz[a,h]anthracene (DBA), Indeno(1,2,3-cd)pyrene (InP), Benzo[a]pyrene (BeP), Benzo[k]fluoranthene (BkFL), Benzo[b]fluoranthene (BbFL), Chrysene (Chr), Benz[a]anthracene (BaA), Pyrene (Pyr), Fluoranthene (FL), Anthracene (Ant), Phenanthrene (Phe), Fluorene (Flu), Acenaphthene (AcP), Acenaphthylene (AcPy), 2-Methylnaphthalene (MeNaP), Naphthalene (NaP), 1,2,3-Trimethylbenzene (TriM). The mean value and standard deviation of 3 independent extractions [N = 3] of 3 independent incineration particles per condition was reported in the figures A-D.

Acute in vitro cytotoxicity response of incineration particles exposed on air-liquid interface cultured lung cells (A549) and macrophages (THP-1) co-culture. (A) MTS- and (B) LDH cell viability assay, 72 h post incineration particle exposures, was performed. The assays (A-B) included a non-exposed negative control (NC 1, set to 100 % viability) as well as a negative exposure control (NC 2) with a steady airflow of 7.8 L air min−1 but no incineration particles. Additionally, two positive controls PC 1; 100 µL 10 mM Cadmium sulfate solution and PC 2; 100 µL 10 µg mL−1 E. coli LPS solution, were included in this study. Blue bars show the viability of plastic coated (PQC and PEC) and uncoated (PUC). In similar fashion the viability of textiles (green; TUC and TQC) and wood incineration particles (yellow; WUC and WEC) is shown. Three independent cell passage experiments and three technical replicates (N = 3, n = 3) were conducted and statistical relevance evaluated by a two-tailed distribution T-test (P-values <0.05 *, <0.01 **, <0.001 ***).

Acute pro-inflammatory cytokine response of incineration particles exposed on ALI co-culture. (A&B) List of pro-inflammatory cytokine analytic of supernatants. The charts (A&B) include both the above-mentioned negative controls NC 1 and NC 2 as well as both positive controls PC 1 and PC 2. The heatmap cluster illustration is showing (red) up-/ (blue) down-regulation fold change in a color code of cytokines (A) and cytokine concentration [pg x mL−1] detected in supernatant (B). Three independent cell passage experiments and three technical replicates (N = 3, n = 3) were conducted.

Fluorescence microscopy imaging of air-liquid interface cultured lung cells (A549) and macrophages (THP-1) co-culture exposed to incineration particles. (B, F, J, N, R) Cytokeratin 19 red staining detected at 550 nm was selected as marker for A549 lung epithelial cells. (C, G, K, O, S) DAPI blue staining imaged at 405 nm was utilized for nucleus staining. (D, H, L, P, T) CD45 green staining detected at 520 nm was selected for macrophage THP-1 staining. (A, E, I, M, Q) are the overlays of the three independent staining's illustrating the confluent cell monolayer with incineration particle present (E, I, M, Q). (A-D) shows the NC 1, whereas (E-H) shows TUC, (I-L) shows TQC, (M-P) shows WUC and (Q-T) shows WEC.
Specification summary of the applied substrates and coatings.

row.entry: hCQDs
Polymer Institute, Slovak Academy of Sciences (SAS)
Coating solution: Hydrophobic carbon quantum dots (hCQDs), blue LED light activated
hCQDs were synthetized by PISAS following the method described (Stanković et al., 2018). The particles were solved in Toluene at a concentration of 1.36 g L−1.
PUC, APP, plastic
Boltaron 9815E, US
Commercial product: Airplane plastic (APP) for interior components of aviation cabins, also referred to as plastic uncoated (PUC)

PVC/Acrylic extruded plastic containing fire retardant and meeting FAA requirements for flammability, smoke generation and heat release under harsh physical properties (9815E - Boltaron® Thermoplastics,). Among others, Flammability FAR 25.853 A1, Heat Release FAR 25.853 A1 and NBS Smoke F814–84 release tested.
PQC
Empa, CH
Functionalized product: Boltaron 9815E plastic spray coated with hCQDs, plastic quantum coated (PQC)
According to coating characterization an amount of 0.0096 mg hCQDs cm−2 (lowest/highest: 0.006 – 0.013 mg hCQDs cm−2) is deposited on the APP. Empa characterization results.
PEC
Evonik, DE
Functionalized product: Boltaron 9815E plastic wire bar coated with Evonik up-converter, plastic Evonik coated (PEC)
According to coating characterization an amount of 9.85 mg up-converter cm−2 (lowest/highest: 9.19 – 10.51 mg up-converter cm−2) was applied. Evonik characterization results.
UPC, E
Evonik, DE
Coating solution: Antimicrobial up-converter (UPC) coating
UPC was synthetized by Evonik following a method described in WO2021073915A1 (Jüstel and Fischer, 2021)
TUC, APT, textile
TISCA 110813–1, CH
Commercial product: MIRA X RAILWAY SBB FLIRT FV 1.KL 9236 textile, also referred to as textile uncoated (TUC) and airplane textile (APT)
This seat cover textile intended for public transportation contains 85 %/15 % WO/PA (Wool/Polyamid) and flame-retardant compounds. MT15068B1, Charge and Colorit: 30977/03 (Tisca,).
TQC
Empa, CH
Functionalized product: MIRA X RAILWAY SBB FLIRT FV 1.KL 9236 textile spray coated with hCQDs, textile quantum coated (TQC)
According to coating characterization an amount of 0.0096 mg hCQDs cm−2 (lowest/highest: 0.006 – 0.013 mg hCQDs cm−2) is deposited on the APT. Empa characterization results.
WUC, W, wood
Unger & Gronke GmbH, DE
Commercial product: Solid wooden plates derived from beech
These wooden plates are representative for a huge variety of using cases, e.g. table plate, wooden handrails or wooden chairs (Tischlerei Unger u u u,).
WEC
Evonik, DE
Functionalized product: Wood wire bar coated with Evonik up-converter, wood Evonik coated (WEC)
According to coating characterization an amount of 9.85 mg up-converter cm−2 (lowest/highest: 9.19 – 10.51 mg up-converter cm−2) was applied. Evonik characterization results.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Airborne transmitted as well as multiresistant pathogens can spread rapidly in high traffic enclosed public spaces. Antimicrobial coatings of surfaces are a promising approach limiting the spread of diseases by targeted contact inhibition of microbes. A novel class of light-activated coatings including hydrophobic carbon quantum dots (hCQDs) and up-converter (UPC) are developed for this purpose including Safe-by-Design (SbD) principles. The efficacy against pathogens and the human safety aspects need to be well balanced. In case of fire-related accidents, these additives should not increase flammability, smoke release or the formation of harmful entities. In this work, antimicrobial hCQDs & up-converters are coated onto porous and non-porous substrates, exploring the interplay between substrates and coatings on the release of fume products after incineration. The airborne soot particles were assessed for their polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbon (PAH) content and their potential to cause a pro-inflammatory cytokine response in human lung cells. No significant acute in vitro lung cytotoxicity was detected from nano-coated surfaces after incineration. With this study, an important intermediate goal is reached and provides the rational for further investigations.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: A systematic approach exploring the interplay between substrates and coatings on the release, including particle size and polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbon content and the acute in vitro lung cytotoxicity during incineration.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Ever since the COVID-19 pandemic, it became apparent that airborne and droplet transmitted pathogens lead to the spread of diseases (Watson et al., 2022; Kim and Kim, 2022; Jones et al., 2023). A variety of measures have been put into action to tackle these issues. These practices included social distancing, point-of-departure testing, face coverings, mass quarantining facilities, and more frequent routine cleaning and disinfections (Jones et al., 2023). Cleaning protocols and cleaning policies applied in public transportation areas are time and personnel intensive (Kim and Kim, 2022; Weiss et al., 2019). Additionally, cleaning agents often contain non-ecofriendly aggressive chemicals and residues that can cause skin irritation (Hwang et al., 2022; Meier et al., 2023). As in all public transportation systems, airplanes accommodate a so-called "Airplane Cabin Microbiome" which was established by over 200 samples taken from 10 different transcontinental US flights and subsequent 16S rRNA sequencing (Weiss et al., 2019). It was found that the Airplane cabin microbiome consists mainly out of non-pathogenic human skin, oral, and environmental generalist bacteria from the classes of Propionibacteriaceae, Enterobacteriaceae, Staphylococcaceae, Streptococcaceae, Corynebacteriaceae, and Burkholderiaceae on both air and touch surfaces (Weiss et al., 2019). In a later study, the number of airborne bacteria and fungi was determined to be ≤ 800 cfu/m3 for bacteria and ≤ 500 cfu/m3 for fungi. Concentrations were determined to be higher in conditions with high humidity (Kim and Kim, 2022). Besides increased cleaning, the application of coated surfaces has been implemented to limit the spread of pathogens in public areas. Novel antimicrobial coatings may contribute to overcoming these limitations. In recent years, two major principles of antimicrobial coatings in public areas have been intensely studied. The first aims for contact inhibition of microbes by applying biocidal active compounds, mostly silver, copper, or quaternary ammonium compounds (Meier et al., 2023, 2022; Yong and Calautit, 2023). The second strategy aims at preventing attachment of microbes on surfaces due to the application of a super hydrophilic or super hydrophobic layer onto the surface (Yong and Calautit, 2023). The latest development includes photoactive antimicrobial entities, which on demand catalyse the production and release of reactive oxygen species (ROS) or can up-convert visible to UV-C light used for ROS formation (Yan et al., 2024; He et al., 2024; Zhou et al., 2023; Tagaras et al., 2024). Photoactive nanomaterials are superior regarding stability compared to small molecules, since they are not prone to photobleaching, degradation and depletion, and their activity can be easily controlled (Yan et al., 2024; He et al., 2024). ROS can oxidize any organic compound or biomolecules of microorganism near the coating rendering them inactive. This is a particularly efficient strategy for antimicrobial treatments (He et al., 2024; Zhou et al., 2023; Tagaras et al., 2024). The up-converted UV-C irradiation can cause antimicrobial inactivation directly by induction of DNA breakage leading to interference with DNA transcription and translation, therefore resulting in mutations and eventually cell death (Yagura et al., 2011). They rather act as catalysts when activated by irradiation and possess long-lasting activity (Yan et al., 2024; Zhou et al., 2023). Two very promising coating systems under development are hydrophobic carbon quantum dots (hCQDs) or up-converter based coatings. Blue LED light-activated hCQDs based coatings (Shaalan et al., 2024; Marković et al., 2019; Ghosal et al., 2021) and a light-activated up-converter (UPC) coating (Jüstel and Fischer, 2021) were applied on PVC/Acrylic plastic, textile seat covers, and wood substrates for incineration experiments. hCQDs nanoparticles produce singlet oxygen when irradiated with blue LED light at 470 nm, whereas the UPC emits UV radiation between 250 and 320 nm when excited with cyan light around 480–500 nm. The UV-C radiation as well as the generated ROS have been proven to be effective against pathogens such as Staphylococcus aureus, Listeria monocytogenes, Escherichia coli, and Klebsiella pneumoniae ( Yagura et al., 2011; Marković et al., 2019; Ghosal et al., 2021). The necessity of novel potent antimicrobial coatings is evident, but despite proven efficacy, the Safe-by-Design (SbD) aspects including hazard, workers' exposure during production, exposure during use, and life-cycle assessment should be considered during the development of novel antimicrobial coatings to meet the EU’s Green Deal objectives (Furxhi et al., 2024). Novel coatings applied in the aviation industry should not increase the flammability, smoke composition and toxicity of the coated substrate (Chaturvedi, 2010; Alarifi et al., 2013). Although fire-related aviation accidents are rare (Chaturvedi, 2010), toxic smoke emissions released from fires are the predominant cause of death in major fire accidents (Alarifi et al., 2013). Over a 26-year period, 95 fire-related civil aviation accidents were reported leading to 2400 casualties (Chaturvedi, 2010). When combusted, cabin interior is reported to release toxic black soot containing PAH and toxic gases including HCN, CO, SO2, NH3 and NO2(Chaturvedi, 2010). The assessment of flammability, total heat release (HRR) and total smoke release (TSR) is therefore industry standard (Alarifi et al., 2013). PAHs contain two or more aromatic benzene rings and are formed when organic matter is incompletely combusted (Zhao et al., 2023; Zhang et al., 2022). Upon metabolization of PAHs by cytochrome P450 enzymes in the human body (Zhang et al., 2022), they are recognized to cause carcinogenic, teratogenic, and mutagenic effects from a long-term perspective and acute effects such as asthma and bronchitis when inhaled and deposited in the respiratory system (Zhao et al., 2023; Cheng et al., 2022). Nowadays, PAHs emissions are closely monitored by toxicologists around the globe (Cheng et al., 2022). These combustion experiments are especially important, since the use of materials in public areas is a highly controlled and regulated area, where every new component like novel coatings undergoes stringent safety assessment (Righi et al., 2020).

hCQDs and up-converter coatings have been studied for their antimicrobial efficacy under laboratory conditions on test substrates such as glass slides (Shaalan et al., 2024; Marković et al., 2019; Ghosal et al., 2021; Jüstel and Fischer, 2021). Whether these coatings remain in their active state when applied on public space substrates such as PVC/Acrylic plastic, textile seat covers, and wood needs to be verified. To our knowledge, due to the novelty of these coatings, it is unknown if the thermal degradation of these coatings alone or in the interplay with the applied substrates catalyse undesired and potentially harmful fume products for humans. Therefore, the nanocoated substrates were incinerated always in comparison to a non-coated control substrate counterpart. We speculate that it is likely that these carbon-based hCQDs may be transformed, at least partly, to carcinogenic PAH products. Therefore, the aim of our study is to explore and understand the interplay between substrates and coatings on the release of harmful compounds such as PAH and the in vitro lung cytotoxicity during incineration. To achieve this aim, we developed a coating procedure for hCQDs and UPC on three representative substrates (plastic, textile and wood). Then we characterized the quality and quantity of coating applied and verified their active state by singlet oxygen monitoring, prior to the standardized incineration. During incineration, the release of PAH, as well as the in vitro lung cytotoxicity of the smoke was assessed. To identify the size distribution and quantity of soot particles and their potential to reach the lung respiratory system by inhalation a DMS on-line particle analytics coupled with a mass quartz-balance was conducted. Finally, the pro-inflammatory factors were assessed to detect potential upregulation and release of cytokines by the direct contact of incineration fume particles with the A549-THP-1 co-culture lung model.

In this study the antimicrobial hCQDs coatings investigated were supplied by Polymer Institute Slovak Academy of Sciences (PISAS) and the UPC by Evonik as specified in detail in Table 1. To mimic the case study as realistically as possible, a certified aviation cabin plastic supplied by Boltaron (non-porous) and a textile seat cover fabric for public transportation supplied by TISCA (porous) were applied as support material for the antimicrobial coating deposition (see Table 1). Additionally, wood was included as a sustainable natural substrate material.

Spray coating was selected as the method of choice for the hCQDs coating since it is frequently applied in the aviation industry and is technically scalable (Sobon, 2018). The methodology was adopted from Yang et al. (2021) with minor modifications. First, the supplied hCQDs coating concentration was determined by fluorescence calibration measurement (FluoroMax-Plus, Horiba Scientific, US). Then coating liquid was added to a solvent resistant spray bottle (Fix 0.5, Birchmeier, CH). Subsequently, the spray applicator with coating was mounted on a tripod stand at a nozzle height of 0.23 m and a horizontal nozzle direction ( Fig. 1A). Coating substrates were placed 0.20 m from the nozzle tip on a paper indicating the spray area (0.42 m length, 0.32 m width). The surface of the substrates was coated four times by pushing the spray injector once each time. After each spray application a duration of 60 seconds was allowed for evaporation of toluene on the substrate before the substrates in the spray area were repositioned (Fig. 1B). For repositioning, coated substrates furthest away from the applicator nozzle were put to the front. Substrates from the front moved to the middle and substrates from the middle moved to the back. This process was repeated a total of four times. By spraying in mass balancing glass vials (WXTE, Mettler Toledo, CH) a deposited hCQDs amount could be calculated, since a UV illumination of the underlying coating paper (Fig. 1C) proved that > 90 % of the coating was applied within the determined coating area. The calculation of the coating deposition is explained in detail in the results section. Finally, a vacuum drying of the coated substrates was performed to completely eradicate the solvent toluene on the substrates.
Coating characterization of hCQDs applied on substrates was performed by SEM imaging, singlet oxygen production analysis and Raman analytics.

was conducted with a SEM (Nova, NanoSEM 230, Thermofisher, US, Fig. 1D) with coupled EDX measurement to further analyse the elemental composition of the substrates. Depending on the substrate charging properties a magnification of 5 – 8,000x, and high voltage (HV) of 5 – 10 kV at currents of 33 – 91 pA were selected for detailed optimized imaging.

was performed with a Raman spectrometer (Senterra, Bruker, Billerica, US, Fig. 1E) applying a UHTS300S_VIS laser with 457 nm excitation wavelength (Laser Power: 8.027 mW; Centre Wavelength: 529.948 nm) and a Zeiss LD EC Epiplan-Neofluar Dic 50x / 0.55 objective at integration time of 0.5 s. The spectrum analysed ranged from 200 – 5200 cm−1. Pre-trials were conducted to determine the hCQDs spectrum, comprising of two peaks at 1350 and 1550 cm−1.

Singlet oxygen production in the hCQDs/toluene colloid and coatings was monitored by EPR spectroscopy. All measurements were performed at ambient temperature using a Varian E-line spectrometer (UK) operating at a nominal frequency of 9.45 GHz. The central field used was 3380 Gauss, Gain 200, Scan range 100, Time 0.5, and modulation amplitude 2. A selective spin trap agent 2,2,6,6-tetramethylpiperidine (TEMP; Sigma-Aldrich, USA) was used to detect the formed singlet oxygen. Mixtures containing 0.18 mL of TEMP and 2 mL of pure toluene as reference (C7H8; mikroCHEM s.r.o., Slovakia) and hCQDs/toluene colloid were prepared and EPR measured. In the case of coatings, 1 × 1 cm samples were immersed into a mixture TEMP and ethanol p.a. (mikroCHEM s.r.o., Slovakia). Here, as a reference only ethanol p.a. and TEMP were used. All samples in the liquid mixture were irradiated by blue LED light overnight. As a light source, a conventional LED lamp (purchased from LEDart s.r.o., Slovakia) was used with a wavelength of 470 nm, a power of 50 W, and a light intensity of 700 μW/cm2 on the sample surface.

UPC coating characterization was conducted by SEM imaging, coating-depended on analysis (chemical resistance, optical properties) and proof of UV radiation by methylene blue bleaching. SEM imaging was performed with a Hitachi TM 4000 Plus, equipped with a tungsten cathode and operated with 15 kV.

was tested regarding water resistance and methylene blue absorption of the coating to ensure the suitability for testing of bleaching of the methylene blue dye. In addition, resistance against sodium hydroxide solution (5 wt%), hydrochloric acid (5 wt%) and ethanol was checked due to their use as cleaning agents. Gloss and visual evaluation were conducted to ensure proper embedding of UPC by analysis of surface roughness (gloss) by BYK Gardener haze-gloss measuring device and homogeneous distribution by visual observation on a transmitting light pad. To ensure the emission of UV radiation out of the coating, a photochemical measurement approach was conducted via methylene blue dye bleaching. Additional photocatalytically active TiO2 is used to convert UV emission of the up-converter into radicals, which then degrade methylene blue and thus leading to lower absorption of an applied droplet. Therefore, a suspension of methylene blue (4 wt% aqueous solution), photocatalytic TiO2 (Aeroxide® P25) and distilled water was applied on the sample and was irradiated by a LED light source for 24 h. The setup is visualized in Supporting Figure S1. After irradiation, the suspension was recovered by pipetting 100 µL into 900 µL distilled water and centrifuged to force remaining particles to sediment. The remaining absorption of the recovered suspension was measured with an UV/Vis- spectrometer (AnalytikJena, Specord 200 Plus) and was compared to an inactive reference sample.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Hazardous fume assessment applying an incineration platform
ce:section.ce:para: For hazardous fume release assessment of coated plastics and textiles in comparison of non-coated materials an incineration platform was developed and applied as previously described (Netkueakul et al., 2022). Plastic and textile samples (10 cm×10 cm, length/width, Fig. 1F) were incinerated in a controlled matter by an FTT cone calorimeter (Fire Testing Technology, United Kingdom), following the ISO 5660 standard, applying a heat flux of 50 kW/m2 (∼750 ◦C, Fig. 1G). The exhaust emissions were collected above the flame by a sampling probe, distributing the exhaust in a pipe system simultaneously to an on-line particle analyser, a particle collection filter, and an in vitro cell culture exposure chamber (Fig. 1H). The on-line particle analyser was composed of a fast mobility analyser (DMS 500, Cambustion, UK) connected to an aerodynamic particle sizer (APS, Model 3321, TSI, Shoreview, USA). Particle collection was performed by a filter (AX1720HD, Lydall, USA) at a steady exhaust flow rate of 8 L/min controlled by a mass flow controller (Aalborg, USA). The in vitro cell culture exposure chamber (Fig. 1H) consists of a platform where a single 24-well cell culture plate can be mounted combined with a quartz crystal microbalance (QCM, openQCM wi2, Italy) for determination of the mass of deposited particles. For morphological imaging of exhaust particles with scanning electron microscopy SEM (Nova NanoSEM 230, FEI Company, USA), silicium wafer grids were placed into 24-well plates. For each coated- and uncoated control plastic and textile three independent combustions experiments (Fig. 1I) were performed each consisting of on-line particle analytics, particle collection, and in vitro cell culture exposure.

PAH analytics was performed according to the procedure described by Wang et al., 2019 (Wang et al., 2019). In short, particles collected with filter were transferred to counter-balanced glass vials to determine the mass of particles deposited on filter. Next, PAH were extracted within 2 mL acetone/hexane 1:1 (v/v) solvent for 30 min in an ultra-sonication bath. Finally, samples were passed through a 0.22 µm PEG filter (XH66.1, Roth, DE) and collected in 1 mL GC vials. An internal standard (IS) comprised of 19 PAH (Agilent Technologies, MSM-100–1) was included for determination of quantity of PAH and retention time. The IS contains the following PAH all in a quantity of 1 mg mL−1: 1,2,3-Trimethylbenzene, 2-Methylnaphthalene, Acenaphthene, Acenaphthylene, Anthracene, Benz[a]anthracene, Benzo[a]pyrene, Benzo[b]fluoranthene, Benzo[ghi]perylene, Benzo[k]fluoranthene, Chrysene, Dibenz[a,h]anthracene, Fluoranthene, Fluorene, Indeno(1,2,3-cd)pyrene, Naphthalene, Phenanthrene, Pyrene, Toluene. Detection took place in a gas chromatography-single quadrupole mass spectrometer (Trace 1300 – ISQ 7000; Thermo Scientific, US) with DB-EUPAH column 20 m x 0.18 mm× 0.14 mm film thickness (Agilent Technologies, 121–9627).

For the safety assessment of hazardous emission during incineration of coated materials an in vitro air-liquid interface (ALI) model was applied using human alveolar pulmonary epithelial cell line (A549, ATCC CCL-185, Lot no. 60,120,896) combined in a co-culture model with THP-1 human leukaemia monocytic cells (ECOCC, Lot no. 13 C011, England). Therefore, both cell lines were maintained at 37 ◦C and 5 % CO2 in an incubator in T75 cell culture flask containing supplemented cell culture RPMI-1640 growth media (Sigma-Aldrich) containing 10 % fetal calf serum (Sigma-Aldrich), 1 % L-glutamine (Sigma-Aldrich) and 1 % penicillin-streptomycin-neomycin (Sigma-Aldrich) solution. For exposure experiments, 2.5 × 105 cells mL−1 within 1 mL apically and 1.5 mL basolateral per Thincerts™ 24-well inserts (PET, pore diameter 3 μm, Greiner Bio-One Vacuette Schweiz GmbH, Switzerland) were seeded to inserts (-168h pre-exposure) for submerged cell cultivation. −84 h pre-exposure, THP-1 differentiation took place in a T-75 flask with a cell density of 4.0 × 105 cells mL−1 RPMI + 200 nM PMA. Medium exchange and THP-1 seeding into inserts containing A549 cells, at a cell concentration of 0.25 × 105 cells mL−1 apically in 1 mL RPMI without PMA was performed −72 h pre-exposure. −24 h pre-exposure, medium exchange and transfer from submerged to air-liquid interface ALI cultivation conditions was initiated. After 24 h, cell containing inserts were transferred to the exposure chamber before incomplete thermal decomposition platform incinerated substrate fume exposure for 10 min took place. Subsequently, the 24-well plates were transferred back to the cell culture incubator and metabolic activity via MTS- and LDH assay was performed 72 h past exposure. Controls exposed to filtered air flow rate (8 L/min) for 10 min within the exposure chamber (negative control, NC2) and 70 μL addition of Cadmium sulfate solution 10 mM to apical side (positive control, PC1) and Lipopolysaccharide cytokine control 10 µg mL−1 (positive control, PC2) were included in this study for comparison.

For staining inserts after exposure were fixed in 4 % paraformaldehyde/0.2 % Triton X-100 (Sigma-Aldrich, Switzerland) for 10 min at RT followed by 3 washes of buffered PBS (Sigma-Aldrich, D8537) similarly as described previously (Aengenheister et al., 2018; Furer et al., 2022). Then ALI-membranes were blocked in 10 % goat serum (Sigma-Aldrich, G6767) and 1 % FBS in PBS at RT for 1 h. Primary antibody staining were performed either with cytokeratin 19 red antibody (Cytokeratin 19 mouse, abcam, ab52625, Netherland, overnight at RT, 1:500, 550 nm), CD45 green antibody (CD45 Krome Orange, Beckmann, B36294, Germany, overnight at RT, 1:2000, 520 nm) or DAPI blue antibody (DAPI, Sigma-Aldrich, D9542, Switzerland, 1 h at RT, 1:1000, 405 nm). Second antibody staining was conducted by Goat anti-Mouse IgG (H+L) Secondary Antibody (Alexa Fluor 555 conjugate, Molecularprobe, A21422, Netherlands, 1 h at RT, 1:300). Fluorescence imaging and Z-stack images were performed by a confocal laser scanning microscope (CLSM 780, Zeiss, Feldbach, Switzerland).

Supernatants from the lung cell co-culture exposure model, 72 h past incineration particle exposures were collected, preserved at −20°C and subsequently sent to Eve Technologies (Alberta, Canada) for further assessment of the pro-inflammatory mediated release of cytokines and chemokines. A human cytokine proinflammatory focused 15-Plex Discovery Luminex Assay® Array (HDF15) was conducted on GM-CSF, IFNγ, IL-1β, IL-1RA, IL-2, IL-4, IL-5, IL-6, IL-8, IL-10, IL-12p40, IL-12p70, IL-13, MCP-1, TNFα. Sample exposure triplicates (N = 3) were assessed.

Statistical analysis was conducted on metabolic activity assays (MTS and LDH). The three independent and three technical replicates (N = 3, n = 3) were assessed by a heteroscedastic, two-tailed distribution T-test. P-values < 0.05 were reported with *, respectively p-values < 0.01 with **, p-values < 0.001 with ***. Mean values and standard deviations (N = 3, n = 3) are reported in the respective figure.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization and functionalization of the coatings
ce:section.ce:para: After coating application, a thorough morphological characterization was performed through SEM imaging and Raman spectroscopy while coatings' activity was assessed by singlet oxygen. From gravimetrical measurements (N = 6, n = 4), a final hCQDs concentration of 0.96 ± 0.34 mg deposited per 100 cm2 substrate area was calculated. The hCQDs coating is composed by small particles > 10 nm making them quite difficult to visualize on through SEM ( Fig. 2) on rough plastic and textile. This finding suggests that the hCQDs are highly dispersed and homogeneously distributed on the substrate surface. Indeed, due to their nano-scale size of 5 nm (Stanković et al., 2018), the oblate spheroid shaped hCQDs may only be detectable by SEM if they form bigger aggregates on the substrate surface. Raman analytics was conducted to verify the presence and homogeneous distribution of the coating on the different surface carriers (see Fig. 2). On the other hand, Raman analysis confirmed the presence of the hCQDs onto the surface, as proved by the presence of the bands at 1351 and 1561 cm−1 associated to the D and G bands of carbon nanostructures and absent in the uncoated substrates (see Fig. 2, B-J, green insets). Various characterization methods were conducted on UPC coated samples. SEM imaging as depicted in Fig. 2 L shows homogeneously distributed up-converter particles within the coating matrix. No agglomeration or settling of particles could be observed. Further visual evaluation shows a homogeneous distribution of the particles on the entire coating surface (Fig. 2 K). The UV emission of the up-converter in the coating was observed via a photochemical bleaching approach with methylene blue and photocatalytically active TiO2. The coating matrix with incorporated up-converter shows a lower absorption at 655 nm (M), the absorption peak wavelength of methylene blue, than the coating matrix with an incorporated similar siliceous particle without up-conversion ability. Supporting Figure S3 shows a high singlet oxygen (SO) production of the hCQDs/toluene colloids (red curve) in comparison with reference black curve. With increasing hCQDs concentration, generation of SO is increasing, but not linear. The SO production can be affected also with the length of irradiation as mentioned previously (Stanković et al., 2018). EPR measurement of non-coated and coated samples, shows a big difference between the used technologies. Non-coated sample, green curve is almost similar as the reference (black curve; Fig. 2 N). These measurements confirm the SO production, and potential antimicrobial effect of the fabricated coatings with hCQDs. SO is a powerful and effective bactericidal agent suggesting a good photo-sterilization activity of the prepared coating (Marković et al., 2019; Stanković et al., 2018; Bae et al., 2022). These results highlight that hCQDs coating is homogeneously distributed on the substrates and remain in their active state, allowing the production of SO.

To assess the potential adverse effect during incineration of the hCQD coated (PQC, TQC) and UPC coated (PEC, WEC) substrates versus non-coated blank substrates (PUC, TUC, WUC), a hazardous fume assessment using Empa's incineration platform (Netkueakul et al., 2022) ( Fig. 3) was conducted. These light activated coatings are still at an early development stage. Therefore, a single heat exposure of 50 kW/m2 was prioritized according to ISO 5660–1, the European specification (prEN 45545–2) for toxicity assessment of railway vehicle components, or the NIST Radiant Furnace method (ASTM E1678) (Östman, 2023; Knez et al., 2022), for the formation of PAH and acute cytotoxicity analysis as first assessments. As stated in the Materials and Methods section, the incineration measurements comprised of an on-line particle analyser (DMS 500, Cambustion, UK) for quantitative particle amount vs size distribution over time (see Fig. 3, left column) and total amount of particle vs size distribution of particles (see Fig. 3, middle column). For plastic, textile and wood no clear difference was observed regarding the amount and size distribution over time of coated versus uncoated substrates. Recent studies investigating the lung toxicity of smoke‑related particulate matter, found that particles released from fire or smouldering combustion were below 2.5 µm in size (Taylor-Blair et al., 2024; Tian et al., 2024; Cheng et al., 2025), which is following the particle size of 20–100 nm reported in this study. UPC coated substrates (PEC – 70 sec., WEC – 40 sec.) showed a slightly different burning behaviour compared to controls (PUC – 30 sec., WUC – 20 sec.), since the coated substrates took longer to ignite and catch fire (see Fig. 3, left column). Although not statistically significant, this observation is still of interest and could be further explored to have finally a double functional, antimicrobial and flame retardant, coating. Fire retardant coatings are frequently applied in the aviation industry, regulated according to Federal Aviation Administration (FAA) Advisory Circular (AC) 20–135 (Righi et al., 2020). Fig. 3, right column, illustrates the total heat release (HRR; kW m−2), total smoke release (TSR; m2 m−2), the CO- and CO2 concentration (%), parameters frequently tested by industry, over the burning period (Alarifi et al., 2013; Righi et al., 2020). Due to the complexity of the stated data in Fig. 3, only plastic PQC shown in the second top row and wood WEC in the lowest row are described in more detail. The quantitative on-line particle analyser detected most particles in PQC ranging 12–200 nm in size, followed by 9–60 nm size particles, 300 sec. after ignition. In the online-particle measurement plot, these ranges are indicated in the red coloured area. By a cumulative summary of total particles, sorted according to size, the highest concentration of particles with 2.3 1011 ± 0.3 1011 total particles featured a mean size of 22 nm. Taking the total smoke release data as reference, the PQC started catching fire 26 seconds past ignition and burning lasted until 350 sec. past ignition when a plateau of 560 ± 61 m2 m−2 of TSR was reached, indicated with a black line. A maximum CO2 saturation of 0.08 % ± 0.01 % is reached within the exhaust fume indicated by the orange line. During controlled incineration a total heat release of 90 ± 4 kW m−2 was measured, grey line. The controlled incineration of WEC revealed the release of two particle fractions, illustrated in the red area of the online-particle measurement, during the burning. Particles in the size range of 4–200 nm were predominant during the period of 60–225 seconds after ignition. The second particle fraction, following the 225 second mark was narrower with a size range of 8–20 nm. This bimodal particle distribution, with a mean size of 16 nm (7.5 1010 ± 2.2 1010 total particles) and 102 nm (3.0 1010 ± 0.7 1010 total particles) was reflected in the size distribution of particles chart, in all three consecutive incinerations. In contrast to WEC, plastic PQC released only a unimodal distributed particle fraction. The sample fire ignition of WEC started at 40 sec. and lasted until the 200 seconds after ignition. Timewise with 324 sec. PQC burned more than double the time of WEC with 160 sec. This is correlated with the buildup of smoke reflected in the total smoke release data until a plateau of 150 ± 12 m2 m−2 is reached, black line, and simultaneously the highest CO2 saturation in exhaust fume with 0.95 % ± 0.05 % is reached, orange line. By observing the released smoke in the exposure chamber, already visually the burning of PQC released more smoke compared to WEC. This is reflected in a 3.5-times higher TSR compared to wood. As expected, towards the middle of the WEC burning period, the total heat release peaked at 550 ± 68 kW m−2 at 115 seconds. Plastic PQC released a 6-times lower HRR when compared to the heat release of wood. As expected, a clear difference between the substrates was observed. Besides, by comparison of the coated vs. non-coated samples no obvious changes in the burning behaviour were observed. By collecting the particle debris from the exposure chamber on a particle collection filter (AX1720HD, Lydall, USA), the PAH content, recognized for their carcinogenic, teratogenic, and mutagenic properties (Zhao et al., 2023), could be further investigated.

It is well known that incomplete combustion leads to the formation of aromatic benzene rings containing harmful PAHs (Zhao et al., 2023). PAHs analytics should verify the presence of PAHs within the soot particles collected during incineration. Additionally soot of coated vs. non-coated samples should be compared to detect potential elevated PAHs concentrations caused by the coatings. Inhalation as well as skin contact and ingestion are recognized as the primary routes of toxic PAHs exposure in humans (Zhao et al., 2023). Therefore, the collected burn particles originating from each burning experiment of coated- and uncoated substrates were extracted separately in acetone/hexane 1:1 (v/v) solvent for 30 min in an ultrasonication bath as described in Materials and Methods chapter 2.6. After quantification of sample extracts and PAHs standards with gas chromatography and coupled single quadrupole mass spectrometer the quantified PAHs were weight-adjusted for each sample separately yielding in a µg PAHs per mg incineration particle concentration. For better visualization the different PAHs were illustrated in a bar chart (see Fig. 4). Here, uncoated control substrates (A - PUC; B - TUC; C - PUC; D - WUC) were compared with coated substrates (A - PQC; B - TQC; C - PEC; D - WEC). In small quantities most PAHs quantified could be detected within incineration particles (see Fig. 4). Recent studies investigating the PAHs content of burn pit smoke confirmed the presence of 16 different PAHs in both wood and plastic materials (Cheng et al., 2025; Wang et al., 2025; Kim et al., 2021). The controlled plastic incineration yielded the highest total PAHs concentration within the shoot. We reported a cumulative PAH content in the range of up to 75 ± 2.4 µg mg−1 (PQC) in plastics. In contrast, the lowest cumulative PAH content was reported for untreated textiles 27 ± 0.6 µg mg−1 (TUC). The detected PAHs concentrations were in a similar concentration range as previously reported for plywood (10 µg mg−1) and for plastics (30 µg mg−1) (Kim et al., 2021). Interestingly, we reported a 19.3 ± 1.4 µg mg−1 increase in total PAHs per soot concentration of hCQD coated substrates (PQC and TQC) in comparison to the uncoated substrate counterparts (PUC and TUC). The latter fact can be due to the uncontrolled decomposition of the hCQD leading to the formation of smaller conjugated hydrocarbons. In contrast the UPC coated substrates (PEC and WEC) yielded similar PAHs concentrations compared to the uncoated substrates (PUC and WUC). As already hypnotized, it was confirmed that PAHs are present in all soot samples collected during controlled incineration. The PAH content was slightly increase within hCQDs coated samples, whereas the UPC coating was not able to alter the PAHs quantities detected. If these incineration particles cause acute lung in-vitro toxicity or an upregulation of pro-inflammatory cytokine factors, as suggested by literature (Zhao et al., 2023; Taylor-Blair et al., 2024; Tian et al., 2024; Cheng et al., 2025), will be further investigated.

According to literature, it is suggested that smoke from flaming combustion can cause lung injury and inflammation, and the biological responses is associated with PAHs levels (Taylor-Blair et al., 2024; Cheng et al., 2025; Wang et al., 2025). Airborne incineration particles containing PAHs originated from burning antimicrobial coated (hCQDs and UPC) and uncoated substrates (plastic, textile and wood), were exposed on an air-liquid interface cultured co-culture lung model (A549) containing additionally macrophages (THP-1 derived) for a duration of 10 min. The deposited amount was quantified with a quartz balance with a surface area of 0.2826 cm2 located within the exposure chamber in triplicates [N = 3] during the burning time of 10 min. The deposited amounts varied within the different burned substrates drastically, but the additional coating was not reflected in an increase or decrease of deposited particles. These findings are expected since a recent study comparing epoxy composites with and without incorporated graphene nanoplatelet did not alter the deposited particle concentration during a similar combustion setting (Netkueakul et al., 2022). Accounting for the surface area of 1 cm2 for an insert, a deposition of 758 ng cm2 ± 210 ng cm2 for plastics, 280 ng cm2 ± 39 ng cm2 for textiles, and 116 ng cm2 ± 18 ng cm2 for woods was calculated. Due to a major accident scenario, it was decided to investigate a homogeneous surface area of 100 cm2 rather than adjusting the amount of incineration particles to an equal level. After exposure of the A549 and THP-1 co-culture model to the incineration particles, the acute lung cytotoxicity was assessed after 72 h with MTS- and LDH viability assay (see Fig. 5 A & B). For complex toxicological studies as described here, it is from upmost importance to include well designed negative and positive controls to set the sample values into perspective. Therefore, we included two negative controls (NC 1 & NC 2) as well as two positive controls (PC 1 & PC 2). NC 1 condition was not exposed to any particles and was set to 100 % viability as an assay control. To ensure that a steady airflow of 7.8 L air min−1 for a duration of 10 min by itself did not affect the cells in the exposure chamber NC 2 condition was included. A potential dehydration of the cells would be quickly recognized by the NC 2 control. 100 µL of a 10 mM Cadmium sulfate solution served as PC1. As intended, the applied Cadmium concentration led to a significant lung toxic effect of 5.5 % ± 3 %, p = 0.001, average viability. The addition of 100 µL of a 10 µg mL−1 E. coli derived LPS solution served as a cytokine positive control PC2. LPS is known to promote cytokine induction within THP-1 cells as an early inflammatory response (Francisco et al., 2021). The included controls verify the sensitivity of the applied air-liquid interface cultured co-culture lung model for assessing the incineration particles of interest. In Fig. 5 A & B, blue bars highlight plastic incineration treatment, whereas green bars show textiles and yellow bars wood incineration particles. To our surprise, the plastics incineration particle treatment seems to cause no significant cytotoxicity, despite their highest soot deposition rate. However, a minor significant cytotoxic effect was observed for textile and wood substrate incineration particles. Neglecting effects ± 10 % viability as experimental deviation, wood particle exposure led to a reduced viability on average of 82 % ± 13 %, p = 0.05, similarly textile particle exposure resulted in an 80 % ± 13 %, p = 0.05 reduced average viability. Whether or not hCQDs or UPC coating was applied on the different substrates did not negatively affect the acute lung cytotoxicity. The results indicate that both antimicrobial coatings (hCQDs and UPC) possess no additional threat when burned during a major accident when applied on the substrates plastic, textile, and wood. Once the coating application is more defined and refers to the product design, a detailed in-depth analysis including different burning temperature, analytics of toxic gas formation and potential long-term studies like repeated dose toxicity, genotoxicity, barrier integrity and oxidative stress would be required.

To assess the acute in vitro cytotoxicity of pure hCQDs exposed on air-liquid interface cultured lung cells (A549) and macrophages (THP-1) co-culture, two different sets of concentrations were assessed (see supplementary information Figure S4 A & B).

The first concentration range was based on the coating amount of 0.962 mg hCQDs applied on the substrates surface area of 100 cm2. To mimicking a partial as well as a complete exposure of the hCQDs on the ALI inserts, four different concentrations of hCQDs were selected ranging from 0.0108 mg to 1.088 mg hCQDs. The second concentration range was based on a more realistic exposure model, where 5 coated airplane seats (coating surface each 0.36 m2) leading to a total surface area of 1.8 m2, in an enclosed room of 1000 m3 (10 m length x 10 m width x 10 m height) volume. It was assumed that 100 % of the coating stayed active. A breathing rate of 6–8 L min−1 (respectively 360–480 L h−1) and an incubation period of 1 h was assumed, to the smoke leading to a target amount of 19.584 µg (in addition to 2 lower and 2 higher concentrations: 195.84 µg, 97.92 µg, 9.792 µg, 1.9584 µg)

The stated concentrations were exposed to the ALI in vitro cytotoxicity assay under the same conditions previously conducted with the soot particles. Figure S4 A illustrates the MTS viability assay of the 10 separately assessed concentrations, whereas Figure S4 B shows the LDH viability assay results. Finally, a COmbined DOsimetry model (CoDo) was conducted with hCQDs parameter as stated in supplementary information Table S1. By applying these parameters, a real-life lung deposition scenario could be calculated and visualized (see supplementary information Figure S5).

Additionally, to the acute in vitro cytotoxicity assessment of the incineration particles, a cytokine profiling (15-Plex Discovery Assay® Array) of the lung co-culture supernatant was conducted to elaborate whether the soot particle could cause a pro-inflammatory response (see Fig. 6 A & B) as suggested by literature (Taylor-Blair et al., 2024; Cheng et al., 2025; Wang et al., 2025). The assessed makers include GM-CSF, IFNγ, IL-1β, IL-1RA, IL-2, IL-4, IL-5, IL-6, IL-8, IL-10, IL-12p40, IL-12p70, IL-13, MCP-1 and TNFα. In Fig. 6 A, the samples were displayed in a heatmap and clustered according to the cytokine profile patterns. An upregulation is indicated in red versus a downregulation in blue colour. In Fig. 6 B, the pg mL−1 concentration is illustrated on the x-axis. In accordance with literature (Zhong et al., 2009), the cytokines predominantly released by A549 lung epithelial cells (IL-8 and MCP-1) were found in all samples in the highest concentrations (up to 6000 pg mL−1, Fig. 6 B). As expected, the positive control 2 (PC 2) containing 100 μL (10 µg mL−1) Lipopolysaccharide (LPS) was able to upregulate the pro-inflammatory cytokines IL-10, IL-12p40 and TNFα, indicating an early inflammatory response towards LPS (Francisco et al., 2021). The most noticeable samples (Fig. 6 A) were wood uncoated (WUC, upregulation of IFNγ, IL-6, IL-12p70, IL-2 and GM-CSF) and plastic Evonik coated (PEC, downregulation of IL-8, upregulation of IL-1RA, IL-5, IL-13 and GM-CSF) at the assessed endpoint 72 h post soot particle exposure. Despite the observed changes in cytokine release, a strong inflammatory response would be expected to elevate cytokines concentrations 10–100-fold in comparison to the unexposed control (NC1 and NC2), by indicate a pro-inflammatory reaction as shown by others (Francisco et al., 2021).

To verify the presence of both cell types (A549 & THP-1) on the co-culture lung model and confirm that a homogeneous cell monolayer has been built with incineration particles present on the lung model surface a fluorescence microscopy study was included. Therefore, for quantitative assurance of the air-liquid interface cultured lung cells (A549) and macrophages (THP-1) co-culture cell monolayer integrity, fluorescence microscopy imaging post 72 h incineration particle exposure were conducted. In contrast to a recent study, applying solely A549 air-liquid interface cultured cells, this model was improved by a THP-1 co-culture and advanced fluorescence microscopy imaging (Meier et al., 2022). For staining, Cytokeratin 19 red (550 nm) was selected since it is predominantly present in epithelial cells and therefore stains selectively A549 lung cells (see Fig. 7, B column). In contrast, CD45 green (520 nm) is a marker present on the membrane of macrophages, which is ideal to stain THP-1 cells (see Fig. 7, D column). For nucleus staining present in both, A549 lung cells and THP-1 cells, DAPI blue (405 nm) was included (see Fig. 7, C column). For comparison a non-exposed control NC 1 lacking incineration particles was included in the quantitative fluorescence microscopy study. The black irregular dots within the images, show the presence of TUC-, TQC-, WUC-, and WEC particles present on the ALI monolayer. By assessing regions where incineration particles were deposited on the cell monolayer it was evident that THP-1 macrophages tend to surround the incineration particles within proximity. Macrophages induce adaptive immunity by gathering and recognizing non-endogenous aerosols present in lung tissue (Xu et al., 2024). A549 lung cells were homogeneously present on the entire insert surface, although fluorescence microscopy focus-issues resulted in darker microscopy spots on the edges of some images. By fluorescence microscopy, as expected, a homogeneous cell monolayer consisting of A549 cells and THP-1 cells with soot particle on top was confirmed.

Light-activated antimicrobial coatings are potent biocidal compounds, which when activated, can form reactive oxygen species (e.g. 1O2, ROS) or UV-C light attacking and disrupting the cell membrane of pathogens. In this paper it was proven that hCQDs and up-converter can be homogeneously coated onto public spaces substrates without reducing their active state and subsequently explore and understand the interplay between substrates and coatings on the release of harmful compounds during incineration. The samples used for this study represent an early development level of potential future applications. Three commonly used substrates were chosen and coated with a reasonable amount of hCQDs and up-converter. For identifying potential critical hazard issues, standardized characterisation methods were selected. The functionality of the coatings was verified by EPR spectroscopy. The hCQDs were able to generate singlet oxygen whereas the up-converter emitted UV radiation which contributes to the antimicrobial properties of these novel coatings as already described in literature.

No severe changes were observed with respect to burning pattern of the antimicrobial coated substrates in comparison to the uncoated substrates. In contrast, the up-converter coating (PEC, WEC) may delay flammability by 10–20 sec., presumably due to the acrylic based resin, where the up-converter is embedded. This potential double function of antimicrobial coating and flame retardancy should be further investigated in a separate approach. After incineration, we could show that both coatings pose no additional threat to an in vitro lung co-culture model in regard of acute smoke cytotoxicity. Therefore, this research is limited to short term acute lung cytotoxicity.

Once the product design is defined, including substrate coating chemistry and amount of antimicrobial applied, further investigations should be executed addressing coating stability and extended burning scenarios with different heat exposures. Further characterisation of released compounds (PAH) and toxic gases (HCN, NOₓ and SO₂) as well as long-term health effects including repeated dose toxicity, genotoxicity, barrier integrity and oxidative stress should be considered additionally to identify potential hazardous effects.

In addition, our quantitative results of released pollutants and potential toxic agents can be integrated and complemented with further analysis of downstream products including incineration residue, ash treatment and wastewater discharge. This will allow a comprehensive understanding of the environmental impact of novel antimicrobial products during a major accident scenario.
